question.promptText: Explore another moment in Anita and Me where prejudice is significant.

question.contextText: Anita and Me by Meera Syal. Candidates answer this part after part a of the same parent question. They should explore another moment from Anita and Me, not reuse the printed extract.

primaryAnswerChain.title: Prejudice Moment

primaryAnswerChain.fitNotes: One moment plus prejudice and method.

sourceDocument.title: Mark scheme - Modern prose or drama

markSchemeItems.text: Part (b) is marked holistically across Levels 1-6 for AO1 and AO2. Level 6 requires sustained critical style, precise textual references, detailed analysis of language/form/structure and consistently effective terminology.

sourceDocument.title: Mark scheme - Modern prose or drama

markSchemeItems.text: Part (b) assesses AO1 and AO2 equally: maintain a critical personal response with textual references and analyse language, form and structure. AO3 is not assessed as a separate objective.

sourceDocument.title: Mark scheme - Modern prose or drama

markSchemeItems.text: Possible moments include families like us moving in; shopping outings; the naming of Tracey's dog; continuation of the extract incident with the family's repeated experience of similar events; Anita's comments on travellers; elders' evening chats including the first Asian Miss World; Sam Lowbridge at the fete or in the television interview.

sourceDocument.title: Mark scheme - Modern prose or drama

markSchemeItems.text: Indicative AO1: responses may explore the spectrum of prejudice from Sam Lowbridge's aggressive abuse to cultural ignoring so extensive that Meena feels like a squashed fly in an imaginary book of Tollington's Sixties Revolution.

sourceDocument.title: Mark scheme - Modern prose or drama

markSchemeItems.text: Indicative AO2: responses may analyse the harsh impact of Sam Lowbridge's shouted racist political comment and figurative aftermath, or Anita's warning about travellers set against Meena's admiration and recognition of shared distrust and prejudice.

Extract 2 is from Brixton Stories, where Ossie has accepted a lift from Apha and is confused and worried by Apha's strange behaviour and comments.

sourceDocument.title: Mark scheme - Modern prose or drama

markSchemeItems.text: Indicative content rewards understanding that DNA presents an innocent third party arrested because of blame-shifting, while Brixton Stories has one on-stage character responsible and one innocent bystander. It rewards comparison of surprise, ignorance, Leah's chaotic questioning, Ossie's disbelief, and structure built around unwelcome news.

topicPath: Anita and Me / The Football Factory
question.promptText: Compare how family life is presented in these two extracts. You should consider: the situations and experiences faced by the characters; how the characters react to these situations and experiences; how the writers' use of language and techniques creates effects.
question.selfContainedPromptText: Read Extract 1 from Anita and Me by Meera Syal, in which it is the day of the Fete and Meena's mother is exhausted by looking after Sunil and does not want to go with Meena. Read Extract 2 from The Football Factory by John King, in which the writer is remembering his childhood on holiday in Southend with his parents and younger sister Sarah, while a dog is chasing birds and the children are having a race. Compare how family life is presented in these two extracts. You should consider the situations and experiences faced by the characters, how the characters react to these situations and experiences, and how the writers' use of language and techniques creates effects.
question.contextText: Question 1: Anita and Me by Meera Syal and The Football Factory by John King. Candidates are told to read the two extracts and answer both parts. They are advised to spend about 45 minutes on part a and 30 minutes on part b. For part a, they should focus only on the extracts rather than referring to the rest of the studied text.

primaryAnswerChain.title: Extract Comparison
primaryAnswerChain.canonicalChainText: context contrast -> paired evidence -> writer methods

sourceDocument.title: Mark scheme - Modern prose or drama

markSchemeItems.text: Part (a) is marked holistically. AO1 and AO3 are equally dominant, with AO2 also assessed; candidates critically compare and contrast texts, referring where relevant to theme, characterisation, context, style and literary quality.

sourceDocument.title: Mark scheme - Modern prose or drama

markSchemeItems.text: Level 6 (18-20): sustained critical style in an informed personal response to both text and task; perceptive understanding of context; consistently perceptive understanding; precise, pertinent, skilfully interwoven references; detailed analysis of language, form and structure; consistently effective terminology; sustained interwoven comparison.

sourceDocument.title: Mark scheme - Modern prose or drama

markSchemeItems.text: AO3 indicative content: feelings within a family are complex and affected by factors such as childhood ignorance or innocence, familial jealousy and the past.

sourceDocument.title: Mark scheme - Modern prose or drama

markSchemeItems.text: AO3 indicative content: family behaviour may look similar but have different motivations, such as Meena wanting to convince her parents she is sorry or earn a meal, while King's narrator protects Sarah from a moral sense that doing otherwise would be bad.

sourceDocument.title: Mark scheme - Modern prose or drama

markSchemeItems.text: AO1 indicative content: compare how the narrators attempt to support and cater to younger siblings, including Sarah being protected from traffic and Meena showing willing as a devoted sister.

sourceDocument.title: Mark scheme - Modern prose or drama

markSchemeItems.text: AO1 indicative content: compare the fathers' attitudes, including Meena's father pushing her into the bedroom and making her feed Sunil, and King's father reassuring both children that they are winners.

sourceDocument.title: Mark scheme - Modern prose or drama

markSchemeItems.text: AO2 indicative content: discuss Syal's metaphor 'anemone mouth', contemptuous phrases such as 'pureed slop', and the 'near impossible task' to show Sunil's effect on family dynamics.

sourceDocument.title: Mark scheme - Modern prose or drama

markSchemeItems.text: AO2 indicative content: discuss syntax in both extracts, including Syal's lengthy sentence ending with Sunil refusing to eat and King's rambling sentences capturing breathless excitement.

topicPath: Never Let Me Go / Into the Water
question.promptText: Compare how sadness is presented in these two extracts. You should consider: the situations and experiences faced by the characters; how the characters react to these situations and experiences; how the writers' use of language and techniques creates effects.
question.selfContainedPromptText: Read Extract 1 from Never Let Me Go by Kazuo Ishiguro, in which Kathy has driven back to Norfolk at the end of the novel and is thinking about Tommy's death. Read Extract 2 from Into the Water by Paula Hawkins, in which Erin has returned to the river where her lost sister was last seen and hopes to talk to Sean, the policeman in charge of the case. Compare how sadness is presented in these two extracts, considering situations and experiences, reactions, and the writers' use of language and techniques.
question.contextText: Question 2: Never Let Me Go by Kazuo Ishiguro and Into the Water by Paula Hawkins. For part a, candidates focus only on the extracts rather than the rest of the studied text.

primaryAnswerChain.title: Extract Comparison
primaryAnswerChain.canonicalChainText: context contrast -> paired evidence -> writer methods

sourceDocument.title: Mark scheme - Modern prose or drama

markSchemeItems.text: Part (a) is marked holistically. AO1 and AO3 are equally dominant, with AO2 also assessed; candidates critically compare and contrast texts.

sourceDocument.title: Mark scheme - Modern prose or drama

markSchemeItems.text: Level 6 (18-20): sustained critical style; perceptive understanding of context; consistently perceptive response; precise and skilfully interwoven references; detailed analysis of language, form and structure; effective terminology; sustained interwoven comparison.

sourceDocument.title: Mark scheme - Modern prose or drama

markSchemeItems.text: AO3 indicative content: sadness is affected by Kathy and Tommy's Hailsham context, their lover/carer relationship and Tommy's death, and by Erin's sister's disappearance, the passage of time and Sean's absence.

sourceDocument.title: Mark scheme - Modern prose or drama

markSchemeItems.text: AO3 indicative content: sadness can be connected with a place as well as a person, including why people return to places that make them sad and what resolution they hope for.

sourceDocument.title: Mark scheme - Modern prose or drama

markSchemeItems.text: AO1 indicative content: compare emotions and reactions, including Kathy's tears but controlled response and Erin's self-pity suggested by the place name 'Pity Me'.

sourceDocument.title: Mark scheme - Modern prose or drama

markSchemeItems.text: AO1 indicative content: contrast whether the narrators are moving on; Kathy does not let fantasy overtake her and returns to what is supposed, while Erin returns to sadness and fails to 'exorcise the demon'.

sourceDocument.title: Mark scheme - Modern prose or drama

markSchemeItems.text: AO2 indicative content: analyse figurative language and symbolism, including Ishiguro's Norfolk landscape as a place where lost things wash up and Hawkins's personified 'hunched and miserable' cottage.

sourceDocument.title: Mark scheme - Modern prose or drama

markSchemeItems.text: AO2 indicative content: discuss Kathy's lack of distinct memory and euphemism around completion, and Hawkins's metaphors and symbols such as 'frosted', 'pale blue' and 'mist'.

markSchemeItems.text: Level 6: sustained critical style in an informed personal response to both text and task; coherent critical style, consistently perceptive understanding, precise and skilfully interwoven textual references, detailed and well-developed AO2 analysis, effective subject terminology, and perceptive/sensitive AO3 context.

AO1: explore Magwitch’s physical condition, such as 'the prisoner’s wet clothes', and the officer’s indifference, 'merely observing'.
AO1: consider the relationship between Pip and Magwitch and Pip’s realisation of the bond between them, 'that was my place henceforth'.
AO1: link to the wider text, such as Magwitch as a convict at the beginning, protecting Pip over the stolen food, acting as Pip’s benefactor, his treatment in court, and his death.
AO2: explore language that dehumanises Magwitch in the eyes of the law, including 'the prisoner' and 'carried down...put on board'.

AO2: consider the love conveyed between Pip and Magwitch, including Pip’s protective love in 'hunted wounded shackled creature', the physical bond of 'held my hand', and 'affectionately, gratefully, and generously'.
AO2: consider contrasts in lexical fields associated with Magwitch, such as Magwitch as convict compared with Magwitch as father figure to Pip.
AO3: show understanding of Dickens’ interest in the criminal justice system and the plight of prisoners in 19th-century England.
AO3: consider the novel as a bildungsroman and Pip’s changing appreciation of what it means to be a real gentleman.

markSchemeItems.text: Level 6 (35-40): sustained critical style in an informed personal response to both text and task; precise, pertinent and skilfully interwoven textual references; detailed and well-developed analysis of language, form and structure; consistently effective terminology; perceptive and sensitive understanding of context.

Level 5 (29-34): convincing critical style in a well-developed personal response; well-selected and fully integrated textual references; thoughtful and developed analysis of language, form and structure; good terminology; convincing contextual understanding.

Level 4 (22-28): some critical style in a detailed personal response showing clear understanding; relevant textual references; some analysis of language, form and structure; competent terminology; clear contextual understanding.

Level 3 (15-21): reasonably detailed personal response showing understanding; some relevant textual references; reasonable explanation of language, form and structure; some terminology; some relevant contextual comments.

Level 2 (8-14): begins to develop a straightforward personal response showing some understanding; some relevant support; simple comments on language, form or structure; limited terminology; some awareness of context.

Level 1 (1-7): a few relevant comments about the text; limited references; a little awareness of language, form or structure; very little terminology; a little implied contextual awareness. 0 marks: no response or no response worthy of credit.

AO1 indicative content: harsh and resentful Mrs Joe with 'a hard and heavy hand' and her role in making Pip sensitive and susceptible to Estella and Miss Havisham; Joe as a true gentleman and family to Pip, 'a sort of Hercules in strength, and also in weakness'; wider family relationships including Miss Havisham and Estella's destructive relationship, Magwitch and Pip, and Joe's unconditional love.

AO2 indicative content: repetition of physical violence 'by hand' in relation to Mrs Joe's parenting; Joe presented as a victim, 'made Joe Gargery marry her by hand'; family created from death or disappearance rather than traditional units, and the motives of adoptive parents.

AO3 indicative content: Dickens' own dysfunctional family life, particularly an absent father; the novel as a bildungsroman in which Pip's maturation and self-discovery show what it means to be in a real family.

markSchemeItems.text: Part (a) is marked holistically out of 20. Level 6 (18-20): detailed and sensitive analysis of language, form and structure; consistently effective terminology; sustained critical style and perceptive understanding; precise, pertinent, skilfully interwoven references; sustained, interwoven comparison.

Level 5 (15-17): thoughtful examination of language, form and structure; good terminology; convincing critical style with some insightful understanding; well-selected, fully integrated references; sustained comparison.

Level 4 (11-14): some analytical comments on language, form and structure; competent terminology; clear understanding in a detailed personal response; relevant references; develops some key points of comparison.

Level 3 (7-10): reasonable explanation of language, form and structure; some terminology; reasonably detailed personal response showing understanding; some relevant references; some explicit, relevant comparisons.

Level 2 (4-6): simple comments on language, form or structure; limited terminology; straightforward personal response showing some understanding; some relevant support; some identification of key links.
Level 1 (1-3): a little awareness of language, form or structure; very little terminology; few relevant comments; limited references; limited if any attempt to make obvious links.

AO2: candidates may contrast Keats's universal or spiritual language for happiness in love, such as star, splendour and eternal, with Cope's everyday language for transforming happiness, such as shopping and a walk in the park.
AO2: candidates may discuss words and phrases linked by happiness and security, including Keats's Pillow'd, soft, sweet and tender, and Cope's happy, peace and contentment, and easy.

AO2: candidates may analyse literary and structural devices such as quatrain rhyme scheme, Keats's oxymoron sweet unrest, the alliterative tender-taken, and Cope's enjambment conveying headlong happiness.

AO1: candidates may compare how both poems present happiness in the relationship as absolute and life-defining, including Keats's life-or-death conclusion and Cope's statement that she is glad she exists.
AO1: candidates may observe that both speakers focus on their own feelings; the loved one's feelings are not mentioned and the personal pronoun I dominates.
Other relevant content should be credited; the indicative content is not exhaustive.

AO2: candidates may discuss figurative language or extended metaphor, including Douglas's personification of death singling the soldier while doing the lover mortal hurt, and Dyment's metaphor of luck at the bottom of the sea.

markSchemeItems.text: Level 6, 30-34 marks: sustained critical style in an informed personal response to both text and task; precise, pertinent and skilfully interwoven references; detailed and sensitive analysis of language, form and structure; consistently effective terminology; perceptive and sensitive contextual understanding.

Level 5, 25-29 marks: convincing critical style in a well-developed personal response; well-selected and fully integrated references; thoughtful examination of language, form and structure; good terminology; convincing contextual understanding.

Level 4, 19-24 marks: credible critical style in a detailed personal response; relevant references; some analytical comments on language, form and structure; competent terminology; clear contextual understanding.

Level 3, 13-18 marks: reasonably developed personal response; some relevant textual references; reasonable explanation of language, form and structure; some terminology; some relevant contextual comments.
Level 2, 7-12 marks: straightforward personal response; some relevant support; simple comments on language, form or structure; limited terminology; some contextual awareness.
Level 1, 1-6 marks: basic relevant comments; limited references; a little awareness of language, form or structure; very little terminology; implied contextual awareness.
AO4 high performance, 5-6 marks: consistent spelling and punctuation accuracy and consistently controlled vocabulary and sentence structures.
AO4 intermediate performance, 3-4 marks: considerable spelling and punctuation accuracy and a considerable range of vocabulary and sentence structures.
AO4 threshold performance, 1-2 marks: reasonable spelling and punctuation accuracy and a reasonable range of vocabulary and sentence structures; errors do not hinder meaning.

AO1 indicative content: candidates may explore Mercutio's care for Romeo, anti-romantic understanding of love, taunting and banter, his role in Romeo attending the Capulet ball, Act 1 Scene 4, Act 2 Scene 3, Act 3 Scene 1, and his death as the first tragic victim of a love he never understood.

AO2 indicative content: candidates may analyse Mercutio's bawdy imagery of conjuring, the medlar tree and pop'rin pear, the line 'if love be blind, love cannot hit the mark', the scene's position between the first meeting and balcony scene, and Mercutio as foil to Romeo.
AO3 indicative content: candidates may explore Renaissance male friendship, fighting and challenges, Mercutio's status as a relative of the Prince, and the political significance of his death.

Extract-based response guidance: where a candidate has not moved beyond the extract, the mark cannot move beyond Level 3; where there is only brief reference to the wider text, the mark cannot move beyond Level 4.

Level 5, 25-29 marks: convincing critical style in a well-developed personal response; well-selected and fully integrated references; thoughtful examination of language, form and structure; good terminology; convincing contextual understanding.

Level 4, 19-24 marks: credible critical style in a detailed personal response; relevant references; some analytical comments on language, form and structure; competent terminology; clear contextual understanding.

Level 3, 13-18 marks: reasonably developed personal response; some relevant textual references; reasonable explanation of language, form and structure; some terminology; some relevant contextual comments.
Level 2, 7-12 marks: straightforward personal response; some relevant support; simple comments on language, form or structure; limited terminology; some contextual awareness.
Level 1, 1-6 marks: basic relevant comments; limited references; a little awareness of language, form or structure; very little terminology; implied contextual awareness.
AO4 high performance, 5-6 marks: consistent spelling and punctuation accuracy and consistently controlled vocabulary and sentence structures.
AO4 intermediate performance, 3-4 marks: considerable spelling and punctuation accuracy and a considerable range of vocabulary and sentence structures.
AO4 threshold performance, 1-2 marks: reasonable spelling and punctuation accuracy and a reasonable range of vocabulary and sentence structures; errors do not hinder meaning.

AO1 indicative content: candidates may explore Scotland as a violent place, Macbeth being praised for battlefield brutality, the irony of later blood guilt, Duncan's murder, Banquo's ghost, Macduff's family, the final reckoning, and the contrast between heroic violence for a legitimate king and later tyrannical violence.

AO2 indicative content: candidates may analyse 'multiplying villainies of nature', Fortune as a rebel's whore, the sword 'smok'd with bloody execution', 'unseam'd him from the nave to th' chaps', heads on battlements, repeated strokes, 'reeking wounds' and 'Golgotha'.

AO3 indicative content: candidates may use relevant context about Jacobean society, warfare, praiseworthy bravery, rebellion, justice with valour armed, and the disruption of natural and political order.

Extract-based response guidance: where a candidate has not moved beyond the extract, the mark cannot move beyond Level 3; where there is only brief reference to the wider text, the mark cannot move beyond Level 4.
